caveats: a: The occupation mix is a three-digit beverage-and-tobacco proxy.
a: General GenAI exposure does not measure current brewery automation or labor substitution.
a: Owner labor and cross-trained small-brewery jobs blur wage-weighted task allocation.
rho: No representative brewery AI-adoption or realized-savings study was found.
rho: Implementation capacity may be weaker at owner-operated firms than at scaled beverage manufacturers.
rho: The estimate excludes autonomous delivery and speculative general-purpose robotics.

e: The frozen firm count uses a food-processing margin bridge that may be unsuitable for taproom and brewpub models.
s5: Closures are not control transfers and may destroy rather than transfer operations.
s5: The owner-age statistic is cross-industry and based on 2018 data.
s5: Brand acquisitions without operating assets may not qualify under the lens.

q: Self-distributed and direct-to-consumer volume may retain more benefit than wholesaler-served volume.
q: Category contraction can turn cost savings into defensive trade spending rather than price cuts.
d5: Brewers Association craft definitions do not exactly match NAICS or the LMM lens.
d5: A single recent year can be cyclical, and survivor cohorts may outperform category totals.
d5: Product mix shifts toward nonalcoholic beer or adjacent beverages may not remain inside the current service basket.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat beer production and supply to distributors, retailers, hospitality accounts, or brand owners through wholesale, self-distribution, contract-production, and shared-brewery arrangements, with some ancillary direct-to-consumer sales.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 312120 combines distribution-focused production breweries with hospitality-focused brewpubs and taprooms. The latter depend on restaurant, bar, and venue labor and customer economics that would make one labor, retention, and demand screen incoherent, so the lens is narrowed to production breweries with material recurring external supply relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospitality-dominant brewpubs and taproom breweries whose economics are principally restaurant or bar operations; brand-only contract-brewing marketers that do not operate a brewery; captive pilot facilities; shell entities; and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. production breweries with recurring external wholesale, self-distribution, private-label, contract-brewing, or alternating-proprietor relationships, including microbreweries and regional breweries with material off-site sales.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader NAICS 312000 occupation mix rather than breweries: production is 22.11%, transportation/material moving 12.33%, office support 4.87%, and sales 12.15% of employment. ILO task research finds clerical roles most exposed to GenAI. I map exposure in sales administration, account support, forecasting, purchasing, inventory, compliance drafting, scheduling, bookkeeping, marketing content, and management analysis, plus narrower vision and optimization uses, onto distribution-focused brewery work. The broad subsector includes unrelated tobacco and visitor-facing beverage activities, while brewery production, cellar, packaging, lab, maintenance, and delivery tasks are substantially physical, making the bridge uncertain in both directions.
caveats: The occupation mix is a three-digit beverage-and-tobacco proxy.
General GenAI exposure does not measure current brewery automation or labor substitution.
Owner labor and cross-trained small-brewery jobs blur wage-weighted task allocation.
a.change_evidence: A current six-digit payroll extract with wages by detailed occupation, paired with task diaries from production, cellar, packaging, quality, sales, finance, compliance, planning, and warehouse staff at at least fifteen LMM distribution breweries, would materially change this value.
a.rationale: A production brewery has substantial physical and sensory work, but its SKU complexity, distributor and retailer coordination, demand planning, sales activity, inventory, quality records, and regulated paperwork create a meaningful digital task layer. The range counts task substitution and avoided hiring within jobs, not replacement of whole brewers, packaging crews, technicians, or drivers.

d5.bridge: The Brewers Association measures industry beer volume, not five-year constant-quality demand for the narrowed LMM production-brewery basket. It reports 2025 craft production down 4.0%, overall beer volume down 5.7%, and 60% of craft breweries declining. I compound a moderated contraction rather than mechanically extrapolating one year, because firm mix, nonalcoholic beer, innovation, and share shifts may soften the decline. Craft data may overstate weakness for differentiated survivors or understate it if retailer rationalization accelerates.
caveats: Brewers Association craft definitions do not exactly match NAICS or the LMM lens.
A single recent year can be cyclical, and survivor cohorts may outperform category totals.
Product mix shifts toward nonalcoholic beer or adjacent beverages may not remain inside the current service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of NielsenIQ or Circana unit and equivalent-volume data by craft segment, channel, brewery size, and nonalcoholic status, combined with TTB taxable-removal and production data, would support a cohort-based constant-quality forecast.
d5.rationale: Recent evidence indicates structural volume pressure rather than simple price inflation: both total beer and craft beer contracted, distribution-focused microbreweries performed worse than onsite models, and capacity utilization was reported at only 55% for craft beer in 2025. A five-year decline is therefore more defensible than flat growth, with the high case allowing stabilization.

e.bridge: The Brewers Association counted 1,994 microbreweries and 275 regional breweries versus 3,525 brewpubs and 3,784 taproom breweries in 2025, but these are establishment counts for its small-and-independent craft population, not the 95 dataset-estimated LMM firms. Microbreweries are defined by at least 75% off-site sales, whereas brewpubs and taprooms have at least 25% onsite sales. I infer that LMM scale selects more heavily toward distribution breweries than raw establishment shares do, while excluding hospitality-dominant models. This likely understates eligibility if larger taprooms have substantial wholesale operations and overstates it if many nominal microbreweries fall below the EBITDA band.
caveats: Craft segment counts are establishments or brewing companies, not necessarily firms in the dataset band.
Onsite sales thresholds do not reveal whether hospitality is economically dominant.
The frozen firm count uses a food-processing margin bridge that may be unsuitable for taproom and brewpub models.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level roster of the 95 margin-bridged candidates with EBITDA, beer production, onsite versus offsite revenue, contract-production revenue, operating licenses, and ownership would directly classify lens eligibility.
e.rationale: The default outsourced-supply lens fits production breweries with repeat external distribution or contract-production relationships, but not the large hospitality-dominant population. LMM scale should make distribution models more common than among the full brewery count, yet the heterogeneous industry warrants only about half eligibility at base.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 312120 ($5,343,950,354) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 312120 ($29,905,825,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $314,183 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 312120); class <5: 1,291 firms, avg revenue/firm $241,439 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,819 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,245 firms, avg revenue/firm $525,410 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $80,125 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,237 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,200,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $183,072 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,034 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,818,149 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $582,268 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,766,232 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,691,850 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,029,212,550 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $156,954,914 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 95

caveats: The estimate addresses required operator involvement, not survival of each independent brand.
Contract brewing can preserve operator-required production while shifting it to a different firm.
State and federal license structures and direct-sales permissions vary.
o.change_evidence: TTB production and removals split by brewery size, contract-production volumes, alternating-proprietor activity, brewery-license exits, and import displacement would quantify how much demand migrates away from LMM production operators.
o.rationale: Beer remains a physical, perishable regulated product requiring an accountable licensed operator to brew, ferment, condition, test, package, record, and release it. Software can support these activities but cannot replace the brewery operator. Some production may shift to larger contract breweries or imports, so not all surviving quantity must remain with an LMM operator of the lens's kind.

caveats: Distribution and franchise rules vary materially by state.
Self-distributed and direct-to-consumer volume may retain more benefit than wholesaler-served volume.
Category contraction can turn cost savings into defensive trade spending rather than price cuts.
q.change_evidence: SKU- and account-level net-price waterfalls, distributor agreements, promotion accruals, gross-margin bridges, renewal or reset histories, and elasticity tests around documented cost reductions would measure how much benefit is retained over five years.
q.rationale: Productivity gains are not mechanically passed through under fixed wholesale price lists, but a shrinking, crowded category and retailer rationalization increase promotion, pricing, and service pressure. Three-tier distribution inserts an independent wholesaler and retailer, while state franchise rules can lock breweries into distributor relationships. Savings should be partly retained through margin and better assortment decisions, but competitive reinvestment and distributor-retailer bargaining limit durable capture.

caveats: No representative brewery AI-adoption or realized-savings study was found.
Implementation capacity may be weaker at owner-operated firms than at scaled beverage manufacturers.
The estimate excludes autonomous delivery and speculative general-purpose robotics.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after labor hours, headcount, service levels, forecast error, out-of-stocks, write-offs, and compliance exceptions from AI-enabled ERP, sales, forecasting, and scheduling deployments at comparable breweries would narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Within five years, breweries can implement AI-assisted sales and operations planning, production scheduling, inventory and purchasing, account support, marketing workflows, compliance preparation, and exception detection. Realization is constrained by legacy brewery systems, fragmented distributor depletion data, weak master data, change capacity, regulated approvals, quality accountability, and the need for people to perform physical work.

caveats: Closures are not control transfers and may destroy rather than transfer operations.
The owner-age statistic is cross-industry and based on 2018 data.
Brand acquisitions without operating assets may not qualify under the lens.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched dataset of brewery license changes, asset purchases, equity acquisitions, SBA loan files, broker closings, and ownership records for the validated LMM production-brewery roster would separate qualifying transfers from closures, family succession, and brand-only transactions.
s5.rationale: A contracting, mature craft market creates both distress sales and consolidation opportunities, while broad employer-business owner aging adds succession pressure. The 2025 craft closure count of 481 and net brewery decline show churn but do not distinguish sales from shutdowns; therefore the qualifying five-year control-transfer probability is bounded below total business exit pressure.

principal_driver: AI can improve complex forecasting, sales, inventory, compliance, and production-planning workflows without replacing licensed physical production.
principal_weakness: A contracting beer category and distributor-retailer bargaining can absorb operational gains.
